title: News
text:
Stay up to date with the latest announcements, training opportunities, and developments from the Psychology Technicians at Wrexham University.
This page is updated on a regular basis with new information about upcoming workshops, open days, and changes to available resources.



📢 No new announcements at the moment
Check back soon for upcoming events, equipment updates, and student opportunities.




On this page, we’ll share:
- Training sessions & workshops (e.g., SPSS, Gorilla, Quarto setup)
- New equipment or software added to the department news history
- Guides and resources that we’ve recently published
- Opportunities for students to get involved in projects or events
- Highlights from past activities and successful collaborations

title: Equipment and Facilities
text:
Available Equipment

Tobii Pro Spark Eye Trackers
Portable, user-friendly devices for high-quality eye-tracking research. Suitable for projects in attention, cognition, and visual behaviour.
Biopac Systems
A comprehensive suite for recording physiological signals such as EDA (electrodermal activity), ECG (electrocardiogram), EMG (electromyogram), and respiration. Ideal for psychophysiology and health psychology studies.
BioRadio
Wireless physiological data acquisition system, particularly useful for mobility studies and projects requiring real-time bio-signal monitoring.
CANTAB (Cambridge Neuropsychological Test Automated Battery)
A widely used computerized system for assessing cognitive functions such as memory, attention, and executive function.
Virtual Reality Equipment
VR headsets and software support for immerse experimental paradigms using the Unity Experimental Framework. Useful in applied psychology, perception studies, and innovative research designs.
High-Quality Audio-Visual Recording
Digital cameras, microphones, and editing tools for experimental recording, behavioural coding, and presentation material.

title: Equipment and Facilities
text:
Booking and Training

Equipment can only be booked in advance via the Psychology Technicians with prior approval from your supervisor.

Training is provided where specialist knowledge is required (e.g., ECG, EDA, Eye Tracking).

Students are encouraged to discuss project needs early so we can plan suitable training and ensure availability of equipment.

For all bookings and enquiries, please contact us at:
📧 psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk

title: About the Psychology Technicians
text:
The Psychology Technicians at Wrexham University provide essential technical support, training, and expertise to staff and students in the Psychology Department.
We ensure that teaching, research, and student projects are supported with the right tools, equipment, and guidance.



We support the Psychology Department in three key areas:

Teaching Support
We prepare, maintain, and run equipment for practical classes, workshops, and open days.
From laboratory demonstrations to experimental psychology tasks, we ensure everything runs smoothly.
Research Support
We help staff and students set up studies, collect data, and manage specialist equipment.
Our expertise covers Biopac, Tobii Eye Tracking, CANTAB, and software such as PsychoPy, R, and Gorilla.
Student Projects
Undergraduate and postgraduate students rely on our guidance for dissertations, independent projects, and experiments.
We provide training, troubleshooting, and technical advice to make sure students can carry out their work confidently.




We’re always happy to support colleagues across the department and to collaborate on interdisciplinary projects. For undergraduate students, we strongly encourage you to discuss your third-year project ideas with us as early as possible. Many projects require careful planning, and we can often design experiments tailored to your specific needs. This may involve building bespoke solutions from scratch or identifying and adapting reliable “off-the-shelf” tools. Early conversations ensure we have the time to create a setup that works effectively and supports your research aims.

JISC Survey development
Gorilla Experimental Framework
3D modelling and animation (Blender)
2D art and animation
C# / C++ Coding within the Unity / Unreal Game Engine
HTML web development, and CSS (including basic Quarto deployment )
Digital photography and video recording
Video editing and post production (DaVinci Resolve)
Virtual Reality
Specialist knowledge of the Biopac, Tobii Eye Tracker and EEG










Psychology Technician


Specialist in data science, experimental design, and supporting advanced research methods. I provide technical expertise to help design, implement, and analyse complex experiments, ensuring robust and reproducible results. My work spans data management, statistical modelling, and the integration of open source tools and software to optimise research workflows. I collaborate closely with students and staff to translate research questions into practical study designs and actionable insights, supporting projects from conception through to final reporting







Psychology Technician


Specialising in providing support with experimental queries, data collection setups, and guidance on managing data for assignments. She works closely with students and staff to ensure that projects run smoothly, offering technical assistance and practical advice at every stage of the research process..








If you’d like to know more, book equipment, or get support for your project, head to our Contact page.

title: About the Psychology Technicians
text: If you’d like to know more, book equipment, or get support for your project, head to our Contact page.

title: Contact Us
text:
We welcome enquiries from students, staff, and external partners who may require technical support or information about the facilities in the Psychology Department at Wrexham University.
If you need assistance with equipment, project support, training, or have requests regarding technical services, please contact us at:
📧 psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk




Students – Please contact us as early as possible if you require technical support for third-year projects, dissertations, or research activities. Early discussions allow us to plan effectively and ensure you have the right equipment and training in place.
Academic Staff – We are available to support teaching, laboratory practicals, open days, and research projects. If your module or event requires technical input, please reach out in advance so we can coordinate.
External Enquiries / Third Parties – All requests relating to collaborations, equipment demonstrations, or technical support for external projects should also be directed to the email above. We will respond or redirect your enquiry as appropriate.





For reasons of academic integrity and operational efficiency, we kindly ask that all requests — whether for student projects, staff teaching support, or third-party collaborations — be directed through our main contact email. This ensures enquiries are handled promptly, consistently, and recorded appropriately.
📧 psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk

title: Welcome to the Wrexham Psychology Tech-page
text: We provide technical expertise, equipment support, and resources to students and staff in the Psychology Department at Wrexham University

title: Welcome to the Psychology Technicians at Wrexham University
text:
What We Do




Equipment


Learn about our available lab equipment, from EDA and eye tracking to Biopac and CANTAB. Booking information and guides included.






Resources


Access training materials, SOPs, and tutorials on tools such as SPSS, R, Gorilla, and PsychoPy.






Support


Meet the technicians, book time with us, and discover how we can help with your research and teaching projects.

title: Welcome to the Wrexham Psychology Tech-page
text:
Explore Our Site
To find what you need, please visit the sections above:

About – learn more about our role and expertise

Equipment – discover the tools and technologies available

Resources – access open-source and academic tools

Students - Wrexham University Final year students can publish here under the SONA platform section
OSF - Open Science Framework and free open source materials provided
Research Methods - An upcoming OSF Teaching materials from Wrexham Universities Research Methods Modules
Contact – get in touch with the Psychology Technicians team
News - get the latest news on training and short courses provided by the department

title: Resources
text:
The Psychology Department is committed to supporting open science, transparent research practices, and the use of professional software in both teaching and independent projects. Below is a selection of recommended resources that are freely available to students and staff. We describe as well how we as the psychology technicians use these tools down below to help our students.




Questionnaire Page
Experimental Page






Quarto A publishing system for creating reproducible documents, dashboards, and interactive reports. Useful for writing, data analysis, and creating professional portfolios. We use this to output a plethora of content for students.
Open Reproducibility Resources
A growing repository and set of tools that ensure transparency in research, demonstrations and teaching materials. Primarily on Gorilla.sc Open Materials.






Python A versatile language for data analysis, statistics, machine learning, and experiment design. Widely supported across psychology and cognitive science research.
R A powerful statistical environment for quantitative analysis, data visualization, and reproducible research.
Julia A high-performance programming language particularly suited for numerical computing, data analysis, and modelling. Widely used in research requiring speed and scalability, and increasingly adopted in psychology and cognitive science applications.






Open Broadcaster Software A free and open source software for video recording and live streaming.
DaVinci Resolve A professional-grade video editing suite with a free version. Suitable for creating teaching material, experiment stimuli, or presentation content.
Audacity An open-source audio editing tool, useful for preparing stimuli and cleaning recordings.
GIMP / Inkscape Free alternatives to Photoshop and Illustrator, excellent for creating figures, posters, and experimental graphics.





Our Psychology Technicians use advanced virtual reality tools to help students design, build, and test experiments:

Unity Experimental Framework – A flexible platform for creating interactive VR experiments and simulations.
Unity & Unity Hub – Centralized management for Unity projects, versions, and collaboration.
Unreal Engine – High-fidelity engine for immersive VR environments and realistic experimental scenarios.
Blender – 3D modelling and animation software to design custom stimuli and experimental assets.






GitHub / GitLab Collaborative platforms for version control and code sharing. Essential for research teams practicing open science.


For further support, guidance, or training recommendations, please contact:
📧 psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk

title: Equipment and Facilities
text:
Specialist Skills
The Psychology technicians are trained in the latest, simulation training

VR/AR/XR Implementations Designing and delivering immersive experiences using Virtual, Augmented, and Extended Reality to simulate real-world environments for teaching, training, and research purposes.
Immersive Simulation & Scenario-Based Learning Developing tailored scenarios and role-play environments that allow students to experience, practice, and reflect on psychological concepts and professional skills in safe, controlled conditions.
Immersive Studio Toolkit Applying advanced studio tools and platforms to create high-quality, interactive content that integrates seamlessly into teaching and experimental setups.
Interactive Wearables & Portable Technology Utilizing wearable devices (such as biometric sensors, VR headsets, and mobile-based platforms) to gather real-time physiological and behavioral data, supporting innovative psychological research.
3D Scanning, Photogrammetry & Digital Twins Capturing physical environments and objects to build digital replicas, enabling highly accurate reconstructions for analysis, training, or experimental immersion.
Motion Capture for Immersive Performance & Interaction Implementing motion capture systems to study movement, behavior, and interaction in both research and applied contexts, bringing realism and precision into immersive simulations. —

title: Upcoming…
text: Upcoming…

title: Psychometric Scales Repository
text:
Psychologists regularly use psychometric scales to collect data about peoples attitudes, personality, perceptions, or behaviour (amongst other things).
The questionnaires, scales, and inventories listed below are a great place to get started on looking for a suitable measure for your research. Although, there are many measures that are listed below. You may find a more suitable measure by conducting a Library Search, using Google Scholar, or by other means. If you come across something interesting that you think we should look at, please let us know!


We do not have copyright authority over some measures and cannot grant permission for use.
We recommend students from Wrexham University to use the “Free for use” scales. If you require a particular scale, then please contact the author(s) of the questionnaire for use permissions. However, please consult your supervisor before proceeding.
The scales are provided solely as a courtesy to help Dissertation students develop their experiments efficiently, to get their projects underway, and provide module leaders resources for their teaching modules. Not all scales are provided and will only be available when Wrexham students, researchers and module leaders provide the appropriate evidence of licensing or permission.
Please note that the scales may contain errors. If you identify any inaccuracies, kindly contact psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk. While every effort has been made to ensure accuracy, these materials are prepared by humans and should be carefully checked before use in any research or teaching capacity.
“Additionally, it is the responsibility of each researcher to ensure the information they use from this repository is accurate and complies with copyright law. For more information about copyright and how it applies to research materials, please see the UK Government’s guidance on copyright: https://www.gov.uk/copyright
Please note: Due to the volume of requests, emails requesting permission for use will not receive a reply. We are also unable to provide author contact information.
If you have further questions, you can contact: psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk



This page organises validated questionnaires into key psychological domains. You can access the Gorilla version of these tasks here: Wrexham PsychTech Open Materials


Total number of scales listed: 181




† Proprietary / license required

We do not have copyright authority over some measures and cannot grant permission for use.
We recommend students from Wrexham University to use the “Free for use” scales. If you require a particular scale, then please contact the author(s) of the questionnaire for use permissions. However, please consult your supervisor before proceeding.
The scales are provided solely as a courtesy to help Dissertation students develop their experiments efficiently, to get their projects underway, and provide module leaders resources for their teaching modules. Not all scales are provided and will only be available when Wrexham students, researchers and module leaders provide the appropriate evidence of licensing or permission.
Please note that the scales may contain errors. If you identify any inaccuracies, kindly contact psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk. While every effort has been made to ensure accuracy, these materials are prepared by humans and should be carefully checked before use in any research or teaching capacity.
“Additionally, it is the responsibility of each researcher to ensure the information they use from this repository is accurate and complies with copyright law. For more information about copyright and how it applies to research materials, please see the UK Government’s guidance on copyright: https://www.gov.uk/copyright
Please note: Due to the volume of requests, emails requesting permission for use will not receive a reply. We are also unable to provide author contact information.
If you have further questions, you can contact: psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk

title: Open Source Materials
text:
Open Source Materials
At the Psychology Department at Wrexham University, we are committed to the values of Open Science. Following the practices set out by the Open Science Framework (OSF) , we aim to make our teaching, research, and experimental resources accessible to both students and the wider scientific community.
This section provides open access materials that you can freely view, use, and adapt:

Gorilla Open Materials – Example experiments, task templates, surveys (English and Welsh) and teaching resources created using Gorilla Experiment Builder.
Open Day Materials – Public engagement resources, activities, and handouts from departmental open days.
Experimental Materials – Experimental code for studies, specific equipment, and technologies will be stored here via GitHub.

By sharing these resources openly, we hope to encourage transparency, collaboration, and innovation in the psychological sciences.

title: Resources
text:
Questionnaire Page
Experimental Page

title: Experimental Repository
text:
Psychologists regularly use experiments to collect data about peoples cognitions, personality, perceptions, or behaviour (amongst other things).
However, there are a number of solutions available for conducting data collection with behavioural tasks, such as the Stroop task, Go/No-Go task, or N-back task. At Wrexham University, we predominantly use 2 solutions; Psychopy and Gorilla.
Each has different strengths and weaknesses, so you should consider which system is best for your research. The video below provides a brief introduction to Gorilla.sc and PsychoPy. We have also listed various experimental tasks.
If you have further questions, you can contact: psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk

An upcoming array of batteries are on their way to be made and gathered as a full list of experimental task (Updated to Task Builder 2) within our Wrexham University Repository page on Gorilla.sc. If you are an Undergraduate student at Wrexham University you will be expected to create your own version of the task.

title: Experimental Repository
text:
Cognitive

Attentional Network Test (PANT): Assess three kinds of attentional processes (in ANT\)
Aimed Movement Task Evaluate Fitts’s law (in fitts\)
Balloon Analog Risk Task Evaluate risk-seeking behavior (in BART\).
Bechara’s Gambling Task Our version of Bechara’s “Iowa Gambling Task”. Choose from four decks, each choice with a cost and each providing reward. Used for tests of executive control (in iowa\)
Berg’s Card Sorting Test (BCST) Our version of Berg’s (1948) Wisconsin Card Sorting Test®, in which the subject sorts multi-attribute cards into piles according to an unknown and changing rule (in bcst\)
Bivalent Shape Task Esposito’s non-verbal stroop analog (in BST\)
Big five personality scale Assess the ’big five personality traits (in scales\bigfive.pbl).
Berlin numeracy test The BNT, a short but valid measure of numeracy (in scales\BNT.pbl)
Brown Peterson Test of short-term memory decay (in brownpeterson\).
Change Detection task Identify a change in a visual scene (in changedetection\) See also LuckVogel.
Choice Response Time Choose the stimulus letter (demo only) (in crt\)
Clock Test AKA the “Mackworth Clock Test”. Watch a clock and see when it skips a beat (in clocktest\)
Comfort Scales Four visual-analytic scales to measure heat-stress-related comfort (in scales\physioscales.pbl)
Connections A trail-making test based on Salthouse et al. (in connections\)
Corsi Blocks An implementation of the classic spatial working memory test (in corsi\).
Device Mimicry Task Recreate the path of a 4-df device (in devicemimicry\)
Digit Span Remember a sequence of digits (in dspan\)
Dot Judgment Determine which of two fields has more dots (in dotjudgment\)
Deese-Roediger-McDermott The classic false memory paradigm (in DRM\).
Ebbinghaus A simple implementation of the Ebbinghaus’s memory learning and re-learning procedure (in ebbinghaus\).
Flanker Task Implementation of Eriksen’s Flanker task (in flanker\).
Four choice response time Wilkinson & Houghton’s 4-choice response time. Respond to a plus sign that appears in one of four corners of the screen (in fourchoice\)
Free recall A free recall list memory task, using the Toronto word pool (in freerecall\)
Generation Example study demonstrating the generation effect. (in generation\)
Global Local A basic version of Navon’s global-local task. This uses a special-purpose function to create the stimuli, which uses the rendered characters in a specified font to create the global/local font image (in globallocal\).
Go/No-go Task A Go/No-go task; a simple continuous performance task (in gonogo\).
Handedness inventory A short scale to judge preference for left versus right hand (in scales\handedness.pbl)
The Hungry Donkey Task A version of Bechara’s Gambling Task for children. The donkey chooses from four doors, each door has a cost and reward in apples. Used for tests of executive control test requiring subject to detect a rare visual stimulus (top or bottom) (in donkey\).
Hick’s law Demonstration A demonstration of Hick’s law, where time to decide increases with number of options (in hicks\)
Implicit Association Test A test of automatic associations between memory representations. Tests association between two sets of binary classifications. (in IAT\)
Item-Order Test Identify whether something has changed in a text string. (in itemorder\)
Letter-Digit Task 9-option version of the ‘code substitution’ task. (in letterdigit\)
Lexical Decision Meyer & Schvaneveldt’s LDT–Determine whether a stimulus is a word or nonword (in lexicaldecision\).
Luck and Vogel Change Detection Luck and Vogel’s multi-object visual working memory task (in luckvogel\).
Manikin Task 3D Object Rotation Task. (demo only; in manikin\)
Match-to-sample task See a grid pattern, then determine if it is the same as a new pattern (in matchtosample\).
Math Processing task Compute a complex math problem in your head (in mathproc\)
Math test A test of different mathematical problems (in mathtest\)
Matrix Rotation UTC test battery matrix rotation. Determine whether a 6×6 matrix is the same (with rotation) as another. (in matrixrotation\)
Mental Rotation Shepard’s mental rotation task. Determine whether two figures are identical, subject to rotation. (in rotation\)
Memory Span A visually-presented click-response memory span task (in mspan\)
Muller-Lyer Illusion Psychometric study of a classic visual illusion. (in mullerlyer\)
N-Back PEBL Dual N-Back test (in nback\)
TLX Workload questionnaire based on NASA-TLX (in scales\)
Numerical Stroop Task An implementation of stroop interference, with either keyboard or vocal response input. (in stroop\)
Object Judgment Determine whether two abstract polygons are identical (in objectjudgment\)
Oddball Task Respond to the oddball in a sequence (in oddball\)
Operation Span Test Memory task requiring mathematical distracter task (in ospan\)
Paired Associates Learn semi-random assocations between terms (in pairedassociates\)
Partial Report Procedure Lu et al.’s (2005) update of Sperling’s iconic memory procedure (in partial-report\)
Path Memory Remember a drawn path (in pathmemory\)
Pattern Comparison Task Determine whether two grids are identical (in patterncomparison\)
PEBL Card-sorting Task Determine which rule (out of 10) is being used to sort cards into two piles (in pcards\)
PEBL Continuous Performance Test Our version of CCPT: Conners Continuous Performance Task. 14-minute vigilance test requiring subject respond to non-matches. (in pcpt)
PEBL Continuous Performance Test-AX CPT-AX. (in pcpt\)
PEBL Compensatory Tracker Similar to Makeig & Jolley’s CompTrack Use mouse/trackball to keep a randomly moving target inside a bullseye. (in ptracker\)
PEBL Manual Dexterity Move a noisy cursor to the target (in dexterity\)
PEBL Perceptual Vigilance Task (PPVT) Our version of Wilkinson & Houghton’s Psychomotor Vigilance Task, A vigilance task used to detect vigilance and sleep lapses. (in ppvt\)
PEBL Trail-making task Connect the dots. (in ptrails\)
Probability Monitor Watch multiple random gauges to determine when one shows a signal (in probmonitor\)
Probabilistic Reversal learning Identify which target is more likely to be correct (in probrev\)
Probe-digit task Waugh & Norman’s test of short-term memory decay (in probedigit\).
Pursuit Rotor A traditional eye-hand coordination task. (in pursuitrotor\)
Random generationGenerate a sequence of random digits; a test of executive function (in randomgeneration\)
Reading span Rememeber a list of letters while reading and judging sentences for sensibility (in readingspan\)
Remote associates task A test of creative problem solving (in RAT\)
Simple Response Time Detect the presence of a visual stimulus, as quickly and accurately as possible in srt\)
Simon Task A S-R compatibility interference test. (in simon\)
Situation Awareness Test Monitor moving objects and identify them when probed (in satest\).
Spatial Cueing Posner’s attentional cueing (spotlight) task.. Given a probabilistic cue of where a stimulus will appear, respond as fast as possible. (in spatialcueing\)
Spatial Priming Respond to a stimulus in a 3×3 grid with location primes. (in spatialpriming\)
Stroop task Stroop’s attention task. Respond to either the color or name of stimuli. Also see Numerical Stroop Task and Victoria Stroop Task. in stroop\
Survey Create a survey. (in survey\)
Sternberg PEBL ‘Sternberg scanning’ task. (in sternberg\)
Symbol Counter Task Garavan (2000) counter task Useful indicator of executive control (in symbolcounter\)
Switcher Task Object search based on switching between up to three dimensions. (in switcher\)
Symmetry span The symmetry span; a complex working memory task requiring spatial memory and pattern interference (in symmetryspan\)
Tapping Tap as quickly as possible for 60 seconds. (in tapping\)
Timetap Tap the speed of a prespecified flash (in timetap\)
Timewall UTC Test battery’s Time wall. Estimate the time when a moving target will reach a location behind a wall (in timewall\)
Tiredness scale A short measure of current tiredness (In scales\tiredness.pbl).
TOAV: Test of Attentional Vigilance Our version of the TOVA®: Test of Variables of Attention. A 22-minute (in toav\)
Tower of Hanoi Traditional problem solving/planning task. Tests ability to make and follow plans in problem solving task. (in toh\)
Tower of London Traditional problem solving/planning task. Tests ability to make and follow plans in problem solving task. (in tol\)
Travelling salesman problem Find the shortest path to visit all points (in tsp\).
Two column addition UTC test battery’s 2-column addition Add three two-digit numbers and respond quickly and accurately. (in twocoladd\)
Typing Type various passages of text. (in typing\)
PEBL Vigilance Task. A novel vigilance task requiring both rest and vigilance periods (in vigilance\).
Visual Search Look for a target in a field of distractors (in vsearch\)
Victoria Stroop Task A short standardized implementation of the stroop test (in stroop\)
Weather Prediction Task (in wpt\)
Word fluency task An implementation of the Thurstone or Chicago Word Frequency/Word Fluency task, a component of the COWAT. (in wft\)

At the Psychology Department at Wrexham University, we are committed to the values of Open Science. Following the practices set out by the Open Science Framework (OSF) , we aim to make our teaching, research, and experimental resources accessible to both students and the wider scientific community.

title: Researcher Requirements
text:
If you are planning to run a study and wish to use the using the WUPSY Network, then please contact psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk.

Home Students
Students can only publish on this site if they meet the credit requirements provided on the SONA page. Once you have completed the minimum credit requirements then you will be able to use this space to share your research on this domain.


Researchers
For researchers who are part of Wrexham University Psychology Network can place their research titles here.

title: Case Studies
text:
The psychology technicians have worked extensively across the department with almost

Psychology Laboratory

Our psychophiosiological laboratory holds several equipment and miscellaneous items to conduct in-person experiments. We hold two experimental cubicles that have full temperature and ventilation control.



Gorilla
The deparment also spends time using Gorilla.sc for teaching across modules and also assisting students who wish to use multi-design research and experimental studies. We also subscribe to using JISC and with their recent 2025 JISC x Gorilla agreement we have efficient functionality across our department to establish well rounded research and launching functional survey x experimental studies.
We also provide some Gorilla Open Materials publicly available and made by previous and current academic staff and psychology technicians.


PsychoPy x Tobii EyeTracking

We have worked on helping students using Psychopy and Tobii for synchronous video and Area Of Interest (AOI) based research. We fully constructed the appropriate modules for their experiments.

title: scales
text:
Psychologists commonly use questionnaires to gather information about people’s attitudes, personality, perceptions, and behaviour.
Although the questionnaires, scales, and inventories listed below offer a helpful starting point, they represent only a small selection of the measures available. You may find a more suitable option by searching the Library, exploring Google Scholar, or using other academic resources.
If you come across a measure you think we should consider adding, please feel free to let us know—we always welcome suggestions.
If you have further questions, you can contact: psychology.technician@wrexham.ac.uk

title: scales
text:
Repository:

Facet Map

title: facet-map
text:
Background Information

Please see the website for more information: https://facetmap.org/



Reference

Irwing, P., Hughes, D. J., Tokarev, A., & Booth, T. (2024). Towards a taxonomy of personality facets. European Journal of Personality, 38(3), 494-515. https://doi.org/10.1177/08902070231200919



Copyright restrictions

CC BY-NC-SA

title: facet-map
text:
Background Information

The Academic Self-efficacy scale is a 20 item questionnaire regarding beliefs about learning.



Reference

M. Jerusalem, & R. Schwarzer. Fragebogen zur Erfassung von “Selbstwirksamkeit. Skalen zur Befindlichkeit und Personenlichkeit In R. Schxarzer (Ed.). Freie Universitaet, Institut fer Psychologie, Berlin, 1981.
Satici, S. and Can, G. (2016). Investigating Academic Self-efficacy of University Students in Terms of Socio-demographic Variables. Universal Journal of Educational Research, 4(8), pp.1874-1880.



Copyright restrictions

CC BY-NC-SA

title: SD3-2014
text:
Background Information

The Short Dark Triad (SD3) is a 27-item self-report questionnaire developed by Jones & Paulhus (2014) to efficiently measure three socially aversive personality traits—Machiavellianism, Narcissism, and Psychopathy—in non-clinical populations. The SD3 was created to provide a brief, reliable, and valid alternative to longer or more clinical assessment tools, making it suitable for research settings where time is limited.



Reference

Jones, D. N., & Paulhus, D. L. (2014). Introducing the short dark triad (SD3) a brief measure of dark personality traits. Assessment, 21(1), 28-41. https://doi.org/10.1177/1073191113514105



Copyright restrictions






















Use Case

Allowed?

Notes













Academic research

✔️ Yes

Use exact wording, cite authors













Teaching slides

✔️ Yes

Not for commercial training













Student papers

✔️ Yes

Cite authors













Gorilla.sc / Qualtrics studies

✔️ Yes

Non-commercial only













Translation

❌ No

Needs permission













Commercial use

❌ No

Must obtain license













Editing/modifying items

❌ No

Items must remain unchanged

title: Resources
text:
Our Psychology Technicians use advanced virtual reality tools to help students design, build, and test experiments:

Unity Experimental Framework – A flexible platform for creating interactive VR experiments and simulations.
Unity & Unity Hub – Centralized management for Unity projects, versions, and collaboration.
Unreal Engine – High-fidelity engine for immersive VR environments and realistic experimental scenarios.
Blender – 3D modelling and animation software to design custom stimuli and experimental assets.
